title: Podman doesn't work at all if XDG_RUNTIME_DIR doesn't exist
body:
### Issue Description

If for one reason or another the directory that `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` points to does not exist, no Podman commands can be executed at all.
```console
$ export XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/does/not/exist
$ podman info
Failed to obtain podman configuration: lstat /does: no such file or directory
```
Given that Podman can find configuration in plenty of other directories, exiting the program and refusing to work seems a bit drastic.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. `export XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/does/not/exist`
2. `podman info`

### Describe the results you received

Podman refuses to execute any command.

### Describe the results you expected

Podman uses the configuration in any of the alternative locations and does work. Maybe a `WARN[0000]` or  `ERRO[0000]` message could be issued.

comments.body:
I think this make sense, having XDG_RUNTIME_DIR pointed at something wrong is a serious problem that should be avoided.

Yes podman works without it being set and fall back to /tmp but we have seen endless of issues with the fall back path. If you have that env set it should be valid otherwise just unset it.

Thank you for taking a look at this issue. Maybe some background for why I'm raising such a strange issue.

I'm trying to deploy podman on an HPC system which uses SLURM as the queuing system. As such, PAM is set up to use `pam_slurm_adopt.so` to prevent users from SSHing into compute nodes that they don't have jobs running on. However, `pam_slurm_adopt.so` conflicts with `pam_systemd.so` which is what is responsible for creating and cleaning up `/run/user/$UID` (and some other stuff).[^1] Unfortunately, something (that I couldn't track down yet) sets `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/run/user/$UID` which doesn't exist, because `pam_systemd.so` is disabled and therefore Podman is unusable.

[^1]: To be fair, there is a workaround described in the [manual  of `pam_slurm_adopt.so`](https://slurm.schedmd.com/pam_slurm_adopt.html) to create `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR`, etc. by hooking into Prolog, TaskProlog and Epilog, but there is no way in hell that the system administrators would even remotely consider deploying this.

I understand that this might not a a trivial fix for you but ultimately this is an incorrectly configured env.
Ignoring the env var might lead to other problems where the runtime dir might be set incorrectly by accident or is deleted by  in which case the fall back might be bad.

So I close this.
